column16: Where did you hear about this application?
column17: If you selected "Organizational Outreach", please specify which club you heard about this application from
column18: If you selected "Generate Referral", please indicate who referred you to apply to this position (First + Last Name)
column19: Are there any other commitments that are not reflected elsewhere in your application?
column20: I understand the commitment and am certain I can make it. *
column21: Please customize your resume to match the unique responsibilities and qualifications required for this role. Then, attach your resume (PDF only). *Your resume should be one page, and should highlight past experiences which make you an excellent fit for this specific role. This will almost certainly require a resume different from the one you use for co-op applications.
column23: If you have been in Generate before, please describe what Generate has meant to you. If you haven't been in Generate, please elaborate on how your professional and/or extracurricular experiences have impacted your growth.
column24: What unique combination of skills and experiences positions you to excel in this role and within a team? In other words: what lets you stand out?
column25: Please describe one technical skill you have developed that you are confident in and one that you would like to learn or improve upon. What motivates you to learn new things and take on challenges?
column26: Tell us about your favorite design style (era, designer, etc.) and describe how it influences you in your own work.

column23: My professional and extracurricular experiences have impacted my growth by allowing me to refine my mindset and attention to detail. For instance, when I collaborated on my image editor Java project with my partner, we began with many errors when running our original code. To overcome this problem, we had to closely read each line of code to identify possible errors and debug them. This process took a large amount of time, but the functionality and success of our final code showed the result of patience and precision. To add on, my work as a technical aide at Parents in Action for Leadership and Human Rights has strengthened my attention to detail. Using computer applications such as Photoshop and Microsoft Excel, tools that I had previously only used for personal projects, in a workplace has caused me to be even more careful of the work I was presenting, as the consequences to any errors were much stronger. I was able to identify areas of my work that needed improvement, and took action on these areas, reinforcing my skills.
column24: Through my work and leadership experiences, I am able to thrive in different types of challenges and environments with my skills in adaptability and collaboration. More specifically, I am able to adapt my thought processes and learn from the different voices in a team, while having some autonomy in my decision making. For example, this occurred when I worked as a technical aide at Parents in Action for Leadership and Human Rights. I was tasked with designing an informational brochure to help promote the business’s services, and this would be the first time my design would be utilized in a professional setting. I initially approached this task by designing using a corporate minimalistic style that I saw in other companies. However, after reviewing feedback I gained from my colleagues and supervisors, I pivoted from my original design to align more with the company’s branding identity while maintaining minimalism’s elements of simplicity and functionality. By making this change, the company’s brand recognition was strengthened and reached the target consumer base. This experience grounds me into my belief that a crucial part of being a successful designer is adaptability and open mindedness.
column25: One technical skill I am confident in is using HTML and CSS structures in web development. Through this skill of mine, I am able to combine my love for customization, creativity, and computing into intentional and aesthetically pleasing layouts. I would like to expand my knowledge of this field by growing my understanding of prototyping UI/UX designs and becoming well versed with design applications such as Figma. Becoming proficient in this skill would allow me to enhance the skills I am already familiar with to create and elevate interactive experiences efficiently. I am motivated to learn new things and take on challenges by the desire to impact other people. This motivation extends beyond web development, from connecting with people through instruments and music to creating smiles on the faces of my friends and family because of handcrafted gifts. I aspire to learn and grow by being involved in purposeful creation.
column26: While it is difficult to pinpoint a specific design style to being my favorite style, I can confidently say that the style of Hayao Miyazaki/Studio Ghibli films deeply resonates with me and my own work. To me, Studio Ghibli is appealing because of the unique character designs. It is notable that each character is designed with intention to align with the vision of these movies, whether it is through the character’s color choices, sizes, and purpose in the story. I am inspired to push beyond the “norms” and allow myself to fuel my creative juices in my work. In addition to the meaningful design and storytelling, I am inspired by how Miyazaki is able to use whimsical, cute, and “innocent” stylistic choices to approach serious topics. For example, “My Neighbor Totoro” tells a story of a family moving to the unfamiliar countryside because of the hospitalized mother. While the main characters are faced with worries and anxiety, throughout the movie, a subtle color palette is used to create a mysterious yet peaceful world, emphasizing the warmth of childlike curiosity. I am inspired by these choices to carry a similar theme to my work, portraying an experience visually calming to the eye.

column23: Throughout my time as an underclassman, I have actively sought to explore creative opportunities that allow me further my technical and soft skill set. My experience as a designer on Scout's Studio team has helped me understand how to work with client expectations under tight deadlines, while iterating on products from start to finish, alongside developers and fellow designers. This experience has furthered my interest in the field of UI UX, allowing me to explore potentials ways to create meaningful interactions between humans and digital products in the health and wellness space. Additionally, I've balanced an ability to exercise creative freedom through my time managing Northeastern InterVarsity's social media with an ability to execute existing brand guidelines for a large sports company like DraftKings. While my experiences have varied in responsibility, they have all contributing to my values of being an empathetic, driven, and communicative designer.
column24: Combined with my experience in digital and physical fabrication as a design major, I've worked on multiple types of projects ranging from 72-hour hackathons to semester-long client-based work, which have pushed me to strengthen not only my skills in Figma but also in collaborating as an effective team member. Working alongside talented developers and designers in Scout has given me an understanding of the hard work that goes into creating digital products while it has also deepened my passion for creating things with an intention to bring innovation to the community. While supporting others, I believe that I can bring a dedicated and creative drive to Generate's software design team as someone who understands the design cycle from the perspective of a designer and developer.
column25: My past experiences have shaped my skills in prototyping, rather through Figma or code, I'm confident that I can support the process from ideation to build-out. I have some experience in creating wireframes, user-flows, and site-maps in Figma while using techniques such as auto-layout and creating components, yet in Generate, I hope to further improve upon those skills to create smoother workflows for myself and my team members. What motivates me to learn new things and take on challenges is the opportunity to improve and use that improvement to give better insights and contribute to a community that cares about innovation and developing meaningful results.
column26: Naoto Fukasawa, a Japanese designer known for his minimalist style and contributions to Muji, has a deep-rooted philosophy in understanding how his products will be used and creating solutions that integrate seamlessly to people's lives. As a designer, I'm drawn to the mix of simplicity and delicacy while keeping in mind that I also want to create smooth experiences for people. A quote by another designer, Joe Sparano follows this same idea. "Good design is obvious. Great design is Transparent." While there's definitely a place for creative expression, I'm constantly inspired in my work by this idea that our designs can be satisfyingly integrated into daily life. As a software designer this helps keep users at the center of my work.

column23: Although I have not been a part of Generate yet, my experience as a WISE mentee helped me learn how valuable mentorship, workshops, and community support are when navigating new opportunities. Being part of a community focused on empowering women in entrepreneurship gave me the chance to learn from many astounding women, helping me build my own confidence and making me think more about what kind of impact that I want to make. I learned how to set goals, seek feedback, and embrace my challenges as opportunities for growth. Furthermore, I have worked in customer service for many years, meeting and interacting with people from diverse backgrounds. This has taught me how to communicate clearly with a team, stay patient, and solve problems efficiently. These experiences have strengthened my work ethic, adaptability, and empathy.
column24: I believe what makes me stand out is my confidence in myself. Some may call this delusion, but I truly think that if you believe you can, you will. Confidence is having the self assurance to do something, and no one can take it away from you. Whether you are part of a team, leading a team, or working on your own, confidence drives you to take initiative, make decisions, and stay resilient in the face of challenges. I can be relied on, and I think that makes me a great addition to anything that I work on. Furthermore, I would say that in my work, I am a bit paranoid. I began working at a young age, and I learned the importance of being meticulous, honest, and perseverant. I take ownership and pride in the smallest details, as those minuscule elements make up a big part of who you are. I think that given my
column25: One technical skill I am confident in is Java and object-oriented programming. Through my personal and school projects, I have developed a strong understanding of maintainable code using OOP principles like encapsulation, inheritance, and polymorphism. Some of my personal projects include classic games, such as Minsweeper, Snake, Piano Tiles, and 2048. I enjoy building systems that are both efficient and organized, and Java allows me to create that.  One skill I would like to improve on is C++, especially in the context of blockchain development. I find blockchain systems intriguing due to its performance and memory control, and I want to better understand its decentralized applications. Part of what motivates me is learning something new every day. It is something that you hear often, but I like to keep an open mind when approaching new things, or even new people. I enjoy learning what someone else is like, what different cultures are like, or any unique ideas in the world; a polymath really. I would like to say that being knowledgeable of numerous aspects helps you understand people and life quite well, and some would call this empathy.  Even when approaching an obstacle, I am curious to see what new solutions come up. Of course it will be hectic, but I think that is what makes uncertainty exciting. Challenges test your ability, which for me is where I grow the most and discover what I am really capable of.
column26: Right now I am redesigning my apartment, and my favorite/goal design style is a blend of Japandi and Scandinavian aesthetics, complemented by principles of Feng Shui. I appreciate Japandi for its minimalism and Scandi for its simplicity, and both focusing on functionality and natural materials. The goal is to create spaces that feel calm, balanced, and inviting. The minimalist approach encourages intentional choices and clarity, while simplicity reduces clutter and distractions. In addition, Feng Shui adds another layer by emphasizing harmony and flow within a space, ensuring that the design supports well-being and positive energy. Together, these styles influence my work by encouraging me to innovate solutions that are not only elegant and efficient, but also mindful of the experience and environment. I strive to work with intention, balance, and a sense of zen that cultivates creativity and comfort.

column23: Although I haven’t joined Generate yet, an experience in my California hometown shaped my growth as both a developer and a teammate. Last summer I volunteered with a Santa Ana food-pantry collective that struggled with volunteer no-shows. I led a small student crew to build a lightweight scheduling site and integrated Twilio texts that ping volunteers 24 hours before their shift. The no-show rate dropped from about 40 percent to under 15, and weekly distributions finally ran on time. Turning a community headache into a tested, documented, and hand-off-ready tool taught me that code only matters when it delivers real results for real people.
column24: My edge starts with math. I have earned straight As in every math and computer science course in college and highschool. When I look at numbers, I don’t just read them but I picture the ideas behind them and see how each graph, limit, or matrix reflects a rule that already exists in the world. That habit of visualizing complexity lets me spot patterns fast and break hard problems into clean steps, whether I am debugging code or mapping a data pipeline. I pair that analytic focus with real-world follow-through, like when I turned a food bank’s volunteer issues into a working scheduling site and text-reminder system that cut no-shows by more than half. Strong problem solving backed by user empathy means I can dive deep into the technical details and still ship tools the team can trust and users will actually love.
column25: I am most confident in object-oriented Java. I can design clear class hierarchies, refactor code without breaking behavior, and back everything up with solid unit tests. The skill I want to deepen next is full-stack web development, especially React on the front end and basic cloud deployment, so that I can own a project from database to polished interface. Curiosity fuels me. Whenever a complex idea clicks, whether it is a calculus proof or a tricky bug fix, I feel a surge of excitement that pushes me to tackle the next challenge and keep expanding my toolkit.
column26: My favorite design tradition is the Swiss or International Typographic Style pioneered by Josef Müller-Brockmann and Armin Hofmann in the 1950s. Its disciplined grids, generous whitespace, and restrained color palettes aim for total clarity, which resonates with how I approach math problems. When I build a screen I start with a twelve-column grid, establish a tight type hierarchy, and let negative space do the heavy lifting so users see the content first and the chrome second. Thinking in grids turns composition into a puzzle of ratios and alignment, letting me apply the same logical reasoning I use in calculus proofs. This style keeps me focused on function, reminding me that good design disappears into a smooth, intuitive experience.

column23: The experiences that have had the most impact on my growth are my experience in my high school Game Design Club and my experience in their marching band. The Game Design Club furthered my interest in computer science and was a large influence behind my choice of major. Not only did I learn a new coding language, but I was also able to pass on some of the things I learned to other members. I was able to witness and experience the game development process through projects scattered throughout the school year. My experience in marching band refined my communication skills as well as my leadership skills. During my time as section leader, I would work with the band directors, instructors, and other section leaders to ensure there was no miscommunication between the band and my section. I also tutored underclassmen on their musicality and technique. All these experiences have developed my person in some way. My time with the Game Design Club made me even more passionate about computer science and my time with my high school marching band refined my communication and leadership skills.
column24: I have a bountiful amount of experience with working with other people on large coding projects. When I participated in Aptitude’s summer program, I was put in a group of two other individuals I did not know beforehand. Together, we developed an AI chatbot that users can interact with and compete against in trivia. Throughout the two-month camp, I gained first-hand experience of how a team operates. We wrote daily reports on how our progress and communicated with each other when conflicts arise.
column25: One technical skill I have developed that I am confident in is Java programming. I have used it since my sophomore year of high school and will continue using it this coming fall semester. It’s a language that I am comfortable with using and I enjoy how versatile and structured it is. On the other hand, one skill I would like to improve is working with Excel. While I understand the basics, I understand it’s a powerful tool for analyzing and storing data. I would like to get a better grasp on Excel’s more advanced features. My motivation for learning new things or taking on challenges is my desire to strive for better. I understand that I am nowhere close to my best, and that understanding fuels my desire to reach my best. By learning new things or taking on difficult challenges, I take one step closer to my best self. Even when I fail, I can use the experience to understand my failures and use it to guide my growth.
column26: My favorite design style is Bauhaus and its core principle: form follows function. I interpret this core principle as designing something that is not only aesthetically pleasing but also serves a purpose. Everything you design needs to have a purpose besides sitting there and looking pretty. For example, when I write code, I find myself opting for clean, readable code over something overly complex or decorative. Similar to the Bauhaus design, I avoid writing unnecessary lines of code that would ultimately harm the efficiency of the code.

column23: Generate gave me the opportunity to work directly with real clients and navigate the fast-paced, ever-changing needs of a real product cycle. Collaborating closely with developers on tight timelines pushed me to be more decisive, efficient, and communicative in my design process. Compared to other clubs I’ve been part of, Generate most closely mirrors what it feels like to be on a real industry team with cross-functional collaboration, stakeholder feedback, and the pressure of delivering high-quality work on a tight deadline. That level of realism has been incredibly valuable in building both my design skills and my confidence. Beyond the hands-on experience, what’s been most meaningful has been the people. The community at Generate feels genuinely different from other extracurriculars I’ve been involved in. Everyone is passionate, driven, and incredibly supportive.
column24: My experience as both a designer and a design lead in Generate across two very different teams has given me a unique ability to adapt my approach based on the needs of the project and team. On one team, I worked in a more hands-off environment that required a high level of autonomy, while on the other, I was deeply embedded in each stage of the product cycle, collaborating closely with developers and stakeholders. These contrasting experiences taught me how to be flexible, proactive, and thoughtful about my role depending on the context. As a design lead, I gained extensive experience communicating with the client, translating their goals into actionable design decisions while balancing user needs and technical constraints. Additionally, my background as a developer allows me to approach design with implementation in mind, which helps streamline collaboration with engineers and results in more practical, buildable solutions.This combination of design leadership, technical understanding, and cross-functional communication helps me contribute meaningfully to any team. Not just as a designer, but as a collaborative problem-solver.
column25: One technical skill I feel confident in is designing and building component systems. I enjoy creating scalable, consistent UI elements and thinking through how they work together across a product. However, one area I’ve consistently found challenging is ensuring visual and experiential consistency across full designs, especially when working collaboratively. Even as a design lead, I struggled to maintain seamless transitions between pages when multiple people were contributing. I’ve realized that improving this comes down to practice, clearer communication, and alignment on shared design standards. I’m excited to continue developing this skill through Generate and other group projects this year.  What motivates me to learn is a genuine curiosity and excitement about new ideas and tools. The  tech world is constantly evolving, and staying curious keeps me engaged and adaptable. Whether it’s taking time to learn a new tool or simply keeping up with trends and discussions in the industry, I see learning as an ongoing part of growth and staying relevant in any team or role.
column26: I tend to gravitate towards cleaner and more modern design styles like the work of Eric Hu. I appreciate how he uses textures and interesting shapes to bring relatively simple designs to life. His ability to merge minimalistic elements with dynamic forms creates a fresh visual experience. I do not have that in my own work right now, however I do aim to be clear in my work and lean towards the minimalist side. I hope going forward that I can incorporate more of his principles into my own design so there is this more “engaging character” to my work. While it is not necessarily timeless and fits into every application, in the correct work it could be extremely effective.

column16: My brother, Charles Kim, a Computer Science major and Generate applicant.
column23: One impactful experience I had was as a social media assistant to my town’s local sewing business, Sew Easy. My primary responsibility consisted of posting twice a week on different platforms. Throughout the three months I was there, I developed strong communication skills by collaborating with the other assistant and communicating professionally with the client. Additionally, I enhanced my creative thinking skills by exploring numerous new ideas, adapting to various platforms, and effectively conveying the brand’s image. Another impactful experience was my time as a customer service representative at Treat Cupcake Bar. I was responsible for taking orders, making cake pops, packaging delicate cakes, and even developing my own cupcake flavor. Through this, I learned how small details make a big difference and how important it is to have attention to detail. After many busy shifts and difficult customers, I also learned how to be patient and approach situations with kindness.
column24: One experience I owe most of my product design knowledge to is the Google UX Design Professional Certificate. This course enabled me to develop a range of skills, including empathizing with users, identifying pain points, generating ideas, prototyping, and conducting usability tests. I also learned how to apply foundational UX concepts, including user-centered design, accessibility, and equity-focused design. In this course, I worked on three projects that helped me put my new skills into practice. While those projects helped me grow independently, my time at Ben & Jerry’s taught me how to collaborate in a team. Working in a fast-paced environment, I quickly learned the importance of clear communication, flexibility, and mutual support. From coordinating with coworkers during busy shifts or jumping in to help someone who needs a hand, I developed a strong sense of teamwork. I was also able to build strong friendships with my coworkers, which created a positive atmosphere and helped me understand the importance of connection in a team setting.
column25: One technical skill I am confident in is wireframing and creating high-fidelity mockups in Figma. I am comfortable translating ideas into functional layouts and iterating based on feedback from the start of the design process to the finish. I’ve used these skills throughout all of my UX design projects to map out user interactions, design clean interfaces, and bring ideas to life. On the other hand, one technical skill I am excited to learn is the design-to-development handoff. Specifically, how to prepare and organize design files in a way that makes it easy for developers to implement. I hope to gain an understanding of how designers and developers collaborate throughout the design process. This drive to learn and grow comes from my goal to expand my perspective and get outside my comfort zone.
column26: My current favorite design style is minimalism. Minimalist design emphasizes clarity, functionality, and simplicity by removing unnecessary content and highlighting only the essential elements. In user experience design, I believe that accessibility should be one of the top priorities when designing a product or service. The minimalist style greatly enhances accessibility by reducing visual clutter, providing intuitive navigation, and improving clarity. While I think maximalist designs with rich content and fast motion are cool and exciting, minimalist design provides human-centered design solutions. Through my work, I aim to apply a minimalist style to prioritize user needs and create designs that will improve the overall user experience.

column23: My professional experiences have impacted my growth by exposing me to a variety of Computer Science (CS) applications and helping me figure out what I want to pursue. In high school, I started connecting with startups and local companies to gain work experience and learn more about CS. Over the past few years, I've had the amazing opportunity to work with a variety of different organizations, where I've contributed to various projects across different fields, gained many new skills, and established a supportive network of mentors. My extracurricular experiences have helped me supplement my academic and professional journey by connecting me with my peers and letting me learn in more relaxed, collaborative environments. I recently took on the role of Event Lead for ACM at Northeastern, and I'm currently involved with CS-related volunteering work over the summer to help the community and hone my skills in a more informal setting. I also recently got connected with Professor Intille, where I work to analyze data relating to loneliness at Northeastern. Through these activities, I've fostered a strong passion for CS and gained soft skills that will help me in the future.
column24: My former and current involvement in extracurricular leadership roles position me to excel as a Software Engineer at Generate. In high school, I served as my high school's Marching Band Head Drum Major, where I acted as a student leader during rehearsals and competitions, and the TED-Ed club president, where I facilitated club meetings and in-school public speaking events. Now, I currently serve as our one of our school's ACM Event Leads, where I plan weekly events, workshops, and Hackathons, and a Speaker Coach for TEDxNU, where I will work with a speaker to help them develop their TED talk. Through these roles, I learned how to work well with teams, communicate effectively, meet deadlines, and complete tasks efficiently. I believe my leadership experience also reflects my sense of commitment, as it shows how I dedicate a lot of time and effort to clubs and organizations I get involved with, and try to contribute as much as I can by taking on additional responsibilities.
column25: One skill I'm confident in is my ability to figure things out on my own. I can pick up new skills quickly and complete tasks without too much guidance. For example, a few weeks ago in my current job, my boss introduced me to a platform I've never used and told me to build something. After playing around with it for a day, I was able to complete the project without any guidance on specific implementation. One skill I'd like to improve on is workload management. I have a tendency to overload myself with work and get overwhelmed later. I sometimes feel the irrational need to prove that I can handle a lot of responsibilities. For example, when I first started at Stateable, I'd give myself unreasonable deadlines to complete my tasks. Eventually, I realized this and simply opted for saying, "I'll get it done as soon as possible." What motivates me to learn and challenge myself is the joy I get out of reaching a new milestone. I find it rewarding and exciting to create something new and have hard work pay off. Especially in the world of Computer Science, there's constantly new technology being released, and I want to be as up-to-date as possible.
column26: My favorite general design style is modern and minimalist. I tend to appreciate organized and efficient layouts with minimal clutter. For example, I really appreciate the concept of Tiny Homes because it represents the idea of maximum output, minimum input, and has the bare minimum parts of a house for someone to live in and feel comfortable. In the context of software design, I similarly keep my layouts simple and only include what's needed. I believe in simple but contrasting colors so that you can easily visualize the whole design (ex., white background, grey buttons, black font), organized and aligned components (ex., all buttons in a straight row), and essentially a design that everyone can understand just by looking without being distracted by unnecessary features or colors. While I do believe creativity and abstract design to be an essential components when differentiating between designs, I honestly find designs most beautiful when they have a simple elegance.

column16: A friend also applying to Generate
column23: Being in FBLA throughout high school, I learned and applied my design skills to win 2nd place for UX Design at the state level for the state of California in 2022.  I was then asked by the club leaders to help mentor the UX Design competitors for the following year, in which two of my mentees got first and second place at the state level.  These two experiences were wonderful, in that they got me to re-analyze what I thought of as good software design, as well as leading a team to understand good design at a fundamental level, so that they could excel in competition.  Drawing and painting from a young age, I had always seen good design as a guiding force for any projects I made.  But now, I apply user-centric design to software to guide my UI decisions to deliver not just intuitive and usable, but also aesthetic frontends that all contribute to the overall UX of software.
column24: The design skills I picked up through FBLA and the projects I have worked on over the past few years, have vastly expanded my technical design skills and my general understanding of what good, user-centric design looks like.  But, I also believe that design of any sort is a collaborative effort.  Through starting a club with two of my friends in high school and growing it through member feedback and club fairs, as well as my tutoring of UX Design competitors at States for FBLA in 2023, I have gained lots of experience working with people, and doing so in an open and collaborative manner.  I see this as being able to give effective feedback and making sure that I learn from the successes and failures of both myself and the people I am working with.  I believe that through this process, we can work as a team that can create genuine progress in any field.  The combination of my technical design skills that I have gathered over the past few years, along with my ability to collaborate effectively with a team, makes me a good candidate for this role.
column25: One technical skill I am good at is creating intuitive user-centric designs.  I have a strong understanding for the way that frontend elements and features such as visual hierarchy, consistency, familiarity to users, and proximity have on a user who is foreign to this new application.  I take into account a lot of features that go beyond just well designed software, such as accessibility which I see as a huge component of high quality UI, as it promotes inclusion regardless of any impairments or limitations that the user may have.  I need to work on choosing and going forward with a design applicable to the app.  I like to have a simple black of grey background, with every few options to click on each page for ease of use.  That is pretty standard for me, however there comes applications I work on, such as Haven in which they feel like they need a little more inviting color and imagery, as opposed to the simple dignified sleek design that I am fond of.  I like learning new things because that means I can build new things.  I love applying what I learn so of course I feel motivated to always learn and grow in all ways I enjoy.
column26: My favorite design style is definitely flat design.  I did have a fad of interest in the more visually appealing designs like claymorphism and glassmorphism, but ultimately flat design aligns the most with my UX goals and vision for anything I work on.  I draw a lot of inspiration from apps like Spotify and Uber.  They keep things simple to navigate and understand and clean to look at.  None of the colors or images used feel out of place or jarring.  All put together, their designs look wonderful, but the combination of UI elements they use appropriately contribute to the user experience in a massive way.  Personally I like to use the darker backgrounds with lighter text and imagery, but the other way can be appealing in some cases.  Additionally these company’s use of visual hierarchy such as scale, contract, and proximity work very well in terms of not just contributing to an overall aesthetic design, but also leading the user’s eye exactly where it is supposed to go to get the information they need, contributing to the user-centric design and overall ease of use.

column19: I am a part of the STEP team
column23: Looking back on my first semester and my first day at the Northeastern Electric Racing Club (NER), I couldn’t have imagined where I’d be today. My growth has been shaped by a combination of NER, the Breakthrough Tech AI Program (BTTAI), and my first co-op experience at the Northeastern ITS department.  At NER, I got the chance—even if just for a bit—to practice coding outside of class, using languages like JavaScript and React. It was also my first time using GitHub, which ended up setting me up for success in my Object-Oriented Design (OOD) course. Through BTTAI, I explored machine learning and improved my Python skills, helping me get more comfortable with a field I had been curious about. And with my co-op as a ServiceNow Junior Developer, I finally saw how my computer science skills apply in the real world.  These three experiences have had the biggest impact on my growth—technically, but also in terms of confidence in my major.
column24: I believe the combination of my Computer Science and Business Administration major, with a concentration in Supply Chain, gives me a unique perspective. My business courses have helped me develop strong collaboration and communication skills, which I believe are key to working well with others; including engineers, clients, and cross-functional teams. Outside of academics, I enjoy vlogging on YouTube and TikTok, where I design my own thumbnails and engage with viewers. This creative outlet has sharpened my eye for design and helped me understand what draws people in visually. I believe this allows me to contribute unique input to UI/UX design discussions and user experiences overall.
column25: One technical skill I’ve developed and feel confident in is Java. I’ve used it in several projects, particularly for building backend functionality and writing object-oriented solutions. Even though taking Object-Oriented Design over just two months was intense, it ended up being one of my favorite classes.  A skill I want to improve is front-end development—specifically HTML, CSS, and JavaScript. I’ve worked on a few front-end projects, like contributing to the NER official website (where I successfully updated an icon), and small assignments in my OOD course. However, I still feel there’s room to grow. Right now, I’m working on building my personal website to sharpen these skills further, and I hope to continue improving by joining Generate.  What motivates me to learn new things and take on challenges is the drive to grow both personally and professionally. I love the satisfaction of seeing hard work come together into something tangible. Tackling problems I don’t yet understand and eventually mastering them is one of the most rewarding parts of tech.
column26: As someone who has been making YouTube videos for about five years—on and off—I’ve gone through many different design styles for my thumbnails and channel layout. Over time, I’ve shifted toward a simpler, more minimalist aesthetic. I used to use a lot of bright colors and text, but now I prefer a cleaner and more straightforward look.  I think this preference for simplicity influences my broader approach to design and presentation. That said, I also understand that design is personal and should reflect the user or audience. For example, this past summer, my sister asked me to create Instagram story posts for our trip. column26: I am inspired by the social design movement, where design leads to social change by moving beyond industrial foundations. Within my social design projects, I’ve found that empathy is the key behind data collection. Human-centered user research has allowed me to personally connect with communities in Boston surrounding topics such as the impacts of climate change, digital and physical accessibility, and stigmatization within mental health. Moving beyond data points and synthesis, I believe a holistic understanding of backgrounds, needs, and hopes of users can create empowering systems.

column26: I'm drawn to Swiss design for its simplicity and emphasis on typography. I especially love its use of geometric shapes and compositions. One skill that I want to work on is being more aware of negative space and incorporating the principle of “less is more” in my designs, which is another quality of Swiss design that I really like. Often times when I feel like something I’m working on looks off but I can’t figure out why, I will look to Swiss design principles and realize I’m overcomplicating my design. Even though a poster or magazine spread may look minimal, the choice of placement for every sparse body of text and graphic on a canvas is purposeful. As one of the most widely used typefaces in modern design, Helvetica is a typeface that embodies the principles of Swiss design, which is successful because of its neutrality. It provides an elegant foundation that lets the content speak for itself. When in doubt, I can never go wrong with using Helvetica.

column26: I am inspired by the Bauhaus movement. While it's known for architecture and fine art, its core principles of form follows function, minimalism, and a focus on essential geometry are highly relevant to modern UI design. It’s centered around the idea that good design should be both beautiful and practical, eliminating unnecessary ornamentation in favor of clear, purposeful elements.  The principles of Bauhaus directly influence my UI design process. For example, I always prioritize functionality. Every element, from a button to a text field, serves a clear purpose. I strive to create clean, uncluttered interfaces that guide the user effortlessly. This focus on utility ensures the user experience is intuitive and efficient. Additionally, the Bauhaus emphasis on clarity and simplicity informs my use of typography and layout. I use a strict grid system to create a balanced, structured feel, and a limited color palette to establish visual consistency. Typography is treated as a design element, where I select minimal fonts, weights, and sizes to create a visual hierarchy. Ultimately, the Bauhaus philosophy encourages me to design with a purpose, focusing on creating a logical, efficient, and aesthetically pleasing experience that helps users achieve their goals.

column23: Generate showed me what real collaboration looks like. Before joining, I thought designers and engineers worked in separate bubbles, but Generate taught me how both teams need to work together from day one to build the process for the product. Watching designers explain user needs while engineers shared technical constraints helped me understand that the best solutions come from these back-and-forth discussions. The community aspect was incredible, everyone genuinely wanted to help each other grow. I was never scared to ask questions or for help or even jump in to help teammates debug something, or brainstorm ideas knowing people would build on them rather than shut them down. That supportive environment made me feel comfortable taking risks and learning faster. Working on projects with mixed designer-engineer teams was eye-opening. I saw how a designer's user research could completely shift an engineer's approach, or how technical limitations could spark more creative design solutions. These moments taught me that collaboration isn't just about being nice to each other, it's about leveraging different perspectives to solve problems better than any of us could alone. Generate fundamentally changed how I approach teamwork. I was able to use these collaborative skills in my co-ops and understand the dynamic between designers and engineers.
column24: My co-op experiences gave me something many designers don't get early on - extensive practice presenting to real customers and stakeholders. I've pitched design solutions to Department of Defense test center personnel, learning to adapt my communication style for audiences ranging from highly technical engineers to users with minimal tech experience. This taught me that great design isn't just about the solution - it's about how you sell the story behind it. What really sets me apart is my research-first mindset combined with collaborative iteration. I don't jump straight into high-fidelity designs. Instead, I dig into user interviews, create careful wireframes, and constantly loop in feedback from engineers, team leads, and other designers. I've learned that my initial idea is rarely the best one - the magic happens when different perspectives challenge and improve it. I'm also skilled at navigating competing stakeholder needs. When engineers told me that drag-and-drop system would be too complex to implement as a form builder, I didn't just accept limitations - I found creative alternatives like the building block system that satisfied user needs while respecting technical constraints. I believe that I’m able to bridge the gap between user needs and technical reality, ensuring everyone's voice is heard while keeping the user at the center of every decision.
column25: I've become highly adaptable with design platforms, specifically mastering both Figma and Sketch. Each co-op threw me into different ecosystems - MORSE used Sketch exclusively while Dawn Foods worked in Figma. Rather than seeing this as a challenge, I embraced learning both deeply, which forced me to understand core design principles rather than just memorizing button locations. I discovered that building component libraries and reusable design systems is universal, whether I'm creating local components in Figma or symbols in Sketch. One area I want to improve is advanced prototyping and animation within these platforms. I can build solid static designs and basic interactions, but I know there are more sophisticated micro-interactions and transition techniques that could make my prototypes feel more realistic and help stakeholders better understand the user experience. What motivates me is honestly the efficiency gains from mastering new techniques. When I learned to build proper component libraries, design updates that used to take hours suddenly happened in minutes. That feeling of "there has to be a better way to do this" drives me to constantly explore new features and workflows. Seeing how small improvements in my process can impact entire project timelines gets me excited to keep pushing my skills forward.
column26: I'm deeply influenced by Dieter Rams and his "good design is as little design as possible" philosophy. His approach of stripping away everything unnecessary to focus purely on function resonates with how I think about digital experiences. This philosophy directly shaped my work at MORSE, where I was designing for DoD personnel with varying tech experience levels. Instead of adding visual flourishes or trendy elements, I focused on creating clean, intuitive interfaces that prioritized user goals. When we initially wanted a drag-and-drop form builder, I advocated for the simpler building block system because it better served our users - even though it was less visually impressive. Rams taught me that every design element should earn its place by serving a clear purpose. In my Dawn Foods payment portal, I applied this by removing any visual noise that might distract from the core task of completing payments. Clean typography, plenty of white space, and purposeful color choices created trust and reduced anxiety during financial transactions. What I love about this approach is that it forces me to really understand user needs rather than just making things look pretty. When you can't hide behind decorative elements, every decision has to be intentional and user-centered. That constraint actually makes me a better designer.

column19: There is only one, which doesn't take too much time: Marketing member in CoSMO
column23: Though I haven’t been in Generate before, my experiences have prepared me to contribute meaningfully to team-driven projects and grow as a user-focused, analytical thinker. During my time as a Marketing Intern at Princeton IT Services, I was responsible for growing the company’s audience and clientele through various means, including social media campaigns, outreach initiatives, and SEO-based new website designs. This position exposed me to a more structured, strategic approach to web and project development. Starting with first using Figma, I learned how to efficiently plan and coordinate my designs, organizing my inspiration through mind maps, mood boards, and charts. This helped me streamline the design process and commit to more user-focused design. Additionally, in effort of producing user-friendly, search-optimized designs, I learned how to look for key words in Google and create SEO optimized content with online tools like Ubersuggest. column24: I believe I bring a unique combination of creativity, leadership and technical analysis through my experiences. column25: One technical skill I’ve developed and feel confident in is front-end web development. In high school, I designed my Girls Who Code Makerfair T-shirt the club continues to use to this day, as well as improved my skills in Canva. Through my internship at Princeton IT Services, I designed and built SEO-optimized web prototypes using tools like WordPress and Figma. This experience not only strengthened my ability to create clean, responsive designs, but also taught me how to align user experience with business goals. A skill I’d like to improve is my proficiency in working with APIs and back-end development. While I’ve been exposed to some of these concepts through coursework and platforms like IBM SkillsBuild, I’m eager to deepen my understanding so I can build more dynamic, full-stack applications that involve my interest in data analysis. What motivates me to learn new things is the satisfaction that comes from applying the skills I learned into projects with tangible, accurate results. Seeing my projects work and my designs in action after constant prototyping and improvements fills me with a sense of pride and excitement to keep building and pushing myself further, especially in team-driven environments like Generate.
column26: One of the design styles I admire most is Material Design, developed by Google under the direction of Matías Duarte. What draws me to it is its clean, intuitive structure—every element exists for a reason, with clear intent and thoughtful use of color and motion. I also deeply resonate with Dieter Rams’ design philosophy of "less, but better," where functionality and simplicity go hand-in-hand. I also use inspiration from websites like Awwards and Dribbble where other top designers share their successful ideas.  These sources strongly influence my approach to design. Whether I’m creating a website prototype or marketing content, I always consider how the user will interact with the interface, and what the design is actually solving. In my internship at Princeton IT Services, I leaned heavily into Material Design principles—using Figma to build responsive, modern layouts with clean typography, consistent spacing, and meaningful animation to guide user flow. My early designs were more cluttered and visually driven, but over time, I’ve grown to appreciate clarity, accessibility, and purposeful restraint. Ultimately, these styles have helped me shift from designing for aesthetics alone to designing for experience—making intentional decisions that serve both user needs and product goals.

column16: Word of mouth from friends and peers
column23: Thirty students staring at pizza with no plates taught me more about leadership than any class has. As the new president of NU London's AI Ethics Society, I was running our introductory event when we realized our event coordinator had forgotten to buy the plates. I was embarrassed standing in front of everyone while they waited. While running around looking for plates, I initially thought he was irresponsible. However, after we found some and everything worked out, I realized that if I were in his position, I wouldn't want someone thinking I was careless over what was honestly just a simple mistake. More importantly, I realized this was as much my failure as his because we hadn't set clear expectations as a team. This experience completely changed how I organized future events. For every subsequent event, I started holding meetings the day before to walk through exactly what would happen and what each person was responsible for. I'd follow up in our group chat the day of, communicating not just my expectations, but what we owed to our members. This incident fundamentally changed how I approach challenges. I learned to assume positive intent and focus on systems rather than blame, making me more thoughtful while collaborating with others and proactive about preventing problems.
column24: Teaching tennis and building an AI agent have nothing in common, but they've both made me curious about understanding what people need. Through teaching tennis to students across all skill levels, I've noticed everyone learns differently. I had one student getting frustrated with our traditional group approach, even though he was picking up fundamentals well. After observing, I realized he was putting extra pressure on himself when paired with his older brother. The head coach and I switched up team dynamics, and suddenly, he started enjoying tennis and improving rapidly. This made me realize how much I enjoy figuring out what's going on beneath the surface and how sometimes it's about changing the environment. Whether it's tennis students or Generate clients, success comes from understanding people's real constraints and motivations, not just delivering what you think they need. This curiosity extends to my technical work. At my current internship, I'm engineering a software tool from scratch, and my favorite part has been presenting initial solutions before coding. My technical background means I can implement solutions, but I'm excited to learn how to design them better through user research while working with Generate's real clients.
column25: I believe the best part of building software isn't the programming, but the moment when you present a design and see if people can understand how they'll use it. The tool I'm building at my internship helps engineers build software more efficiently. As an engineer myself, I can visualize how they'll actually use it, then map out both user flow and system architecture simultaneously. This helps me ensure that my design works for real people while being technically feasible. What drives me to keep learning is seeing real problems that affect people's daily lives and knowing I can create solutions. When I built my first project in high school, predicting coronary heart disease and presenting it at a science fair, I realized how rewarding it is to discuss work that solves real problems. This motivation is why I'm eager to learn Figma and create more sophisticated interactive prototypes. Currently, using Canva for flowcharts feels limiting because it doesn’t feel efficient to create each design by hand. I want tools that let me quickly translate ideas into proper interactive prototypes that better communicate user experiences to stakeholders and team members.
column26: Speed and simplicity should be core design features and not just technical requirements. I'm influenced by apps like Real Sports, which delivers live sports scores faster than any other app I've used. As someone who follows sports, I appreciate how they treat instant data delivery as their main selling point. When you open the app during a game, you get real-time scores immediately without loading screens or ads. Their design is clean and focused, letting you drill down into detailed stats if you want, but never forcing complexity on casual fans. When building Forkflow, I applied this same philosophy. I surveyed people on LinkedIn about meal planning challenges and discovered that most college students face the same time and decision fatigue issues I experienced abroad. Instead of assuming what features they wanted, I mapped out how they actually approach meal planning and realized they needed something that works with short attention spans. Just like Real Sports gets you the score instantly, I designed Forkflow to provide meal suggestions without lengthy forms. I used interactive sliders for calorie preferences instead of text boxes, creating familiar flows rather than forcing users to learn new patterns.

column16: Generate Referral, Organizational Outreach, Social Media
column23: Some of my most formative growth has come from being the only designer in environments where design wasn’t the main focus, yet it became my lens for understanding how things (and people) fit together.  At Kappa Theta Pi, I led design for our upcoming recruitment app and learned how to work without a roadmap, from scoping problems, making decisions fast, and getting input from people who didn’t speak “design.” At Kaleidoscope, the Khoury Student Council Club, I built brand systems and campaign visuals on the marketing team, helping coordinate events across 5+ student tech orgs.   In both roles, working solo taught me to own my design decisions, and of how they shape the product and serve its goals. I’ve learned to navigate across roles, speak the language of team leads and engineers, and advocate for design in rooms where no one else speaks it. But that only goes so far without a team to push back. I want to design alongside people who challenge my assumptions and push the work beyond what I could build alone. I’m confident Generate is where I can grow that skill and turn my solo instincts into team-driven strength.
column24: Coming from a Computer Science and Design background allows me to approach projects with a dual perspective. When I design, I’m actively considering technical feasibility and how each decision maps to implementation.  At my current product design internship where I’m working on a presentation AI tool, I’ve been working across the full stack from scripting backend prompt logic in Python to building out the frontend UI. In one project, I was tasked with designing a prompt refinement panel that allowed users to adjust tone, audience, and intent simultaneously. While the concept offered more user control, it ran into technical limitations with our current model. Acknowledging this constraint, and in alignment with the product roadmap, we simplified the flow and reserved the original concept for a future iteration.  This process also pushed me to communicate more clearly across roles. In the very same project, when engineering raised concerns, I worked closely with them and our ML lead to understand the technical trade-offs, then revised the UI to better reflect what was feasible in the current system. Moments like that taught me to welcome critique without defensiveness, listening for the reasoning behind the feedback and responding collaboratively. It allows me to pivot without hesitation, because my design choices are grounded in evidence rather than ego.
column25: One technical skill I’ve grown confident in is interactive prototyping, especially in Figma. I’ve used it to simulate finished products, but more importantly, as a way to test ideas early and often. Prototyping allows me to uncover weak spots, like where a concept falls apart or when it doesn’t hold up in real-world context. That feedback loop is essential to how I design.  One area I’m actively working to improve is motion design. I’ve been diving into After Effects and Premiere to explore how motion guides attention or signals intent. Especially with emerging platforms like AR and VR, motion becomes a core part of how interactions are felt and interpreted. I’m interested in using motion as a way to make systems feel more alive.  What drives all of this is a deep curiosity about how things come together; to understand why they work and how they make people feel. I’m constantly motivated by the gaps, the “what ifs,” because I believe that’s where the most interesting design work lives, and that’s what keeps me learning and building.
column26: My favorite design style is a mix of what I call “romantic maximalism” and “feminine baroque”, a design style that has elements of calligraphic typography and exaggerated scripts that weave through layouts, sometimes bordering on dramatic. Visually, it pulls from things like neo-baroque embellishments (floral curls, gilded frames, ribbon motifs). While it can be overly ornate for some, what pulls me in is how it uses visual texture to heighten emotional response.  In my own work, that influence isn’t always literal; I recognize that the vast majority of projects I work on do not need flourishes and frills. But I still pull from its ideology and emotional language, which is design as an emotional interface. I think a lot about how to create tone, how to evoke feeling without spelling it out. Even in functional product work, I look for opportunities to add softness, small details that make the experience feel more personal and intentional. Those details might seem minor, but they shape how users feel about the product. To me, that emotional impact is just as critical as usability.

column23: My previous roles as a designer in Scout and at Ganbei have taught me a lot in terms of crafting a design targeted towards the audience and working as a team. At Ganbei, I challenged myself in a different way by proposing bold ideas and pushing visual boundaries in different ways. I experimented with different layouts, colors, and more. I learnt to experiment and not to be afraid of iterating my work to find one that fits. column24: As a designer, not only do I utilize my creativity visually, but through problem solving, too. column25: I'm very acquainted with the Adobe Suite (PS, AI, AE, ID). I’ve used these tools to create a variety of designs ranging from ready-to-print layouts all the way to web interfaces. column23: While I haven't participated in Generate before, my experiences have shaped how I approach design as a catalyst for solving real problems. Working on Ping, my flood disaster management app, taught me that design isn't just about interfaces but creating systems that function under extreme stress when lives depend on clear communication. This experience revealed how critical user research is and that assumptions about behavior can be dangerous. Real-world testing with emergency responders showed me insights no theory could provide. My growth accelerated through Code-4-Community, where I designed digital solutions for Boston nonprofits while navigating organizational constraints and advocating for accessibility. This work pushed me beyond aesthetics toward solutions that genuinely serve underrepresented communities. Co-Op Research at Northeastern on faculty data interactions reinforced that users' actions reveal needs beyond what they articulate, requiring careful observation and iteration. Generate's mission excites me because it offers the chance to apply this human-centered approach to support founders facing genuine business challenges where thoughtful design can drive meaningful impact. The opportunity to work alongside diverse teams while helping startups solve complex problems aligns perfectly with my belief that great design emerges from deep understanding and continuous refinement.
column24: My combination of rigorous research methodology, technical collaboration skills, and high-stakes design experience positions me uniquely for Generate. Leading Ping's development taught me that usability in crisis situations demands absolute clarity. Every interface decision required understanding emergency responders' workflows and designing for speed and accuracy when confusion could cost lives. This experience showed me how to balance user needs with technical constraints under pressure. At Code4Community, I learned to advocate for users while working within nonprofit constraints and collaborating across diverse teams. My Northeastern research involved building Tableau dashboards based on actual faculty workflows, not assumptions. This project required synthesizing complex data into actionable insights while maintaining visual clarity. Having worked closely with engineers and contributed to development myself, I understand what enables smooth handoffs between design and implementation. Beyond technical skills, I bring strong facilitation abilities from leading cross-functional teams and conducting stakeholder interviews. This combination allows me to conduct research that uncovers real user needs, translate findings into clear prototypes using Figma and Framer, and communicate effectively with technical teams to ensure designs are both ambitious and buildable for Generate's founders.
column25: I excel at advanced prototyping in Figma and Framer, creating interactive demonstrations that clearly communicate user flows and technical specifications to development teams. My strength lies in conducting user interviews that reveal actual behaviors rather than stated preferences. For instance, surveying over 1,200 Tunul users uncovered their strong preference for step-by-step directions over map navigation, directly informing our design decisions. I consistently apply Nielsen's usability heuristics throughout my design process to ensure functionality from concept to launch. While I prioritize accessible design principles in all my work, I want to deepen my expertise in creating interfaces that seamlessly serve users with diverse abilities without compromising anyone's experience. This requires mastering advanced accessibility patterns, testing methodologies, and staying current with evolving WCAG guidelines. I'm particularly interested in learning how to conduct more inclusive user research and incorporate assistive technology considerations earlier in the design process. I'm motivated by the gap between current digital experiences and human potential. The most rewarding moments come when users complete tasks effortlessly, proving that good design becomes invisible when it truly serves its purpose. Generate offers the perfect environment to push these boundaries while supporting founders who share this vision for meaningful, user-centered innovation.
column26: I draw inspiration from Dieter Rams and Jakob Nielsen, whose philosophies create the foundation of my approach. Rams' principle that "good design is as little design as possible" drives my visual restraint, while Nielsen's usability heuristics ensure that minimal designs actually work for real users. This combination means every interface element must justify its existence both aesthetically and functionally. Their influence taught me that true elegance comes from solving problems efficiently rather than adding superficial flourishes. Nielsen's insight that users scan rather than read has fundamentally changed how I structure information hierarchy and content flow. His emphasis on usability testing reinforces that beautiful design means nothing without user validation. When designing navigation or interactive elements, they must pass Nielsen's criteria for user control and error prevention while maintaining Rams' honest, unobtrusive aesthetic. My process always begins with heuristic evaluation and ends with Rams' question: does every element serve the user's goal? This results in work that balances visual elegance with empirically validated usability, creating experiences that feel effortless because they're built on solid principles rather than trends. Generate's focus on practical innovation aligns perfectly with this philosophy of purposeful, human-centered design that prioritizes substance over style.

column26: My experience in user research and testing has taught me that the user's needs drive the most effective design style! During my summer UI/UX project and time at Generate in Apprenta, I discovered that a design's success largely depends on how well it reflects the desires, themes, and needs identified through user research. That said, if I were to indulge in a personal design preference, I've always been drawn to a style reminiscent of cozy coffee shops and Studio Ghibli films. I love minimalism, retro aesthetics, and warm color grading, but ultimately, my work is shaped by user-driven design principles!

column23: My professional and extracurricular experiences have taught me how to move between vision and execution, balancing user needs, technical feasibility, and business goals. Working across different teams, from startup founders to large corporate stakeholders, I’ve learned to adapt my process, advocate for users, and design with empathy, collaboration, and curiosity. Leading the design for a mobile app taught me how to drive a project from research through handoff, while navigating feedback and advocating for design clarity alongside engineers and a CEO. In a corporate setting, I gained a deeper understanding of what it means to design for accessibility and how to utilize performance metrics to inform iteration and prioritization of tasks. Designing health communication materials challenged me to think critically about how people absorb information in various presentations. These roles pushed me to become more confident in my design decisions, more collaborative in cross-functional settings, and more intentional about impact. Even outside of design, participating in service with Alpha Phi Omega and in teaching roles, I’ve strengthened my empathy, patience, and ability to communicate clearly. column24: I bring a unique intersection of technical knowledge and user-centered design thinking, shaped by both academics and hands-on project work. As a computer science and design major, I’ve built a solid foundation in programming (Java, JavaScript, TypeScript, Python, HTML/CSS, React) while also honing my design skills through courses in UX, HCI, and Information Visualization.
