column25: One technical skill I feel confident in is creating high-fidelity, interactive prototypes in Figma. Through my projects, coursework, and professional experience I’ve learned how to design intuitive user flows, build scalable design systems, and use prototyping to communicate both interaction and intent to engineers and stakeholders. I’m currently focused on strengthening my development skills, especially using React and working with RESTful APIs and databases. I want to better understand how different software solutions are built and be able to contribute more effectively to implementation. Bridging the gap between design and code is something that excites me. What motivates me most is seeing the impact of my work not only on clients and users, but also on my own skillset and perspectives. What’s been especially rewarding is seeing designs move from early ideas to real, functioning products. Being involved in product development across the full lifecycle from research and prototyping to handoff, deployment, and use has shown me how much I can grow when I’m part of a process that challenges me to think and adapt at every stage. In turn, my own growth allows me to craft experiences that better anticipate and respond to users’ needs, ultimately yielding more impactful solutions.
column26: I’m drawn to the Bauhaus movement, because of how it touched art, craft, and technology in a novel and fun, yet evidence driven and logical way. Its principle of “form follows function” reminds me to prioritize purpose and user needs above all else. This has shaped my design philosophy to be highly intentional and user-centered, ensuring that every visual element serves a clear role in enhancing usability and communication. Also as evidenced by the Bauhaus movement, being intentional and evidence-based doesn’t mean design has to be dull or predictable. Its innovative use of geometry, color, and materials created engaging, exciting work that still felt grounded and purposeful.

column23: One of my earliest and most impactful project experiences came from building my first personal website using HTML and CSS in Eclipse. I learned how to build from scratch and it unlocked a whole new world of ideas I hadn’t considered before! Specifically, it led me to create my first app and I’m proud of the platform my team and I built for users to manage their wardrobe and continue to grow their virtual closet. These projects gave me the foundation and confidence to take on bigger challenges, such as my first hackathon, where my team built a budgeting site that targeted spreading financial literacy and participate in the Oasis project series, where my team created a site to track sustainability on Northeastern’s campus by comparing individual and dorm consumption. Experimenting with a simple personal website kickstarted so many new experiences that fueled my hunger for knowledge and kept me motivated to solve problems. The chance to collaborate with other driven students to develop real products for real clients is such an exciting use of creative freedom and I can’t wait to make ideas come to life with Generate!
column24: I pride myself on my perceptiveness! I love picking up on the tone and energy of the project I’m working on and transforming it into a visual concept. This instinct turned into a mini-passion when I taught myself how to edit videos and deemed myself a professional YouTuber whenever someone's birthday came around. There’s something I love about compiling video collages that reflect someone’s energy or turning class projects into dramatic short films. This hobby led me to be chosen as the Production Chair for Northeastern Junoon, where I get to curate the visual elements that support our performances. Right now, I’m editing our introduction video, which sets the stage for our team’s story, but I also get to bring that vision to life by designing the set and costumes. I’m eager to convey our project’s goals through designing deliberate interfaces and accessible products! What sets me apart is my sharp attention to detail and open transparency. I love the process of taking team goals and turning them into something real and I can’t wait to bring a vision to life! Feel free to check out some old videos at your own risk: https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCCLVl9deePL6qlEuVWZXpCw
column25: Web development is one of my strongest technical skills and has quickly become one of my favorite skills to build! Working on Northeastern Electric Racing’s website, Finishline, I’ve had the opportunity to explore such a large project and tackle tickets to solve front-end and back-end issues for the site. Contributing to these projects gave me the chance to learn new languages, like JavaScript, which was a big step up from my early web development experience using just HTML and CSS. Experiences like these motivate me to continue expanding my capabilities with web development and always think creatively about how users interact with the product. One skill I’d like to improve is CAD and 3D modeling. I’m especially interested in learning how to create accurate and visually clear product models that can be integrated into websites to better showcase designs. It’s a skill I’ve seen used across projects, and I’d love to be able to contribute in that way as well. I value expanding my knowledge base knowing I’m making helpful and impactful results. I look forward to stepping into unfamiliar territory when it means I can support my team better and push a project closer to its full potential!
column26: One of my biggest design inspirations is the animation style of Spider-Man: Across the Spider-Verse! I loved when I could spot an easter egg, without the movie explicitly telling me what was going on, through subtle background sound and color changes. For example, the movie shows how alternate-reality Miles has a stronger accent, reflecting his Puerto Rican mother’s influence and subtly signaling how he differs from the original character, who doesn’t know spanish as well. Details like these make people feel more immersed in the movie and it was an overall more memorable watching experience because of it. I admire how the film trusts the audience to pick up on these cues and I prioritize “showing, not telling” in my own work. I try to make meaningful design choices, with color and deliberate wording, that immerse the viewer but is still subtle in its message. It’s refreshing watching the Spiderverse movies because they have a unique approach to telling their story and it inspires my goal to make the viewer feel something first, then notice the details that made them feel that way.

column23: This summer, I had the opportunity to study graphic design and typography in Switzerland. It was my first time traveling out of the U.S., and the experience expanded my perspective on design. Having the opportunity to study at the Basel Academy of Art and Design allowed me to embrace the minimalism of Swiss design. I now embrace grids as a guideline for any good design work, and let that restriction force me to be creative.
column24: I bring a balance of data-driven thinking and human-centered design, which stems from my background as a data science and design major. This allows me to move comfortably between technical constraints and creative exploration. I’ve also had experience as a design intern collaborating with others to develop or improve products. I’m not scared to try new experiences that challenge my skills and abilities.
column25: I'm confident in using Figma to prototype and iterate quickly. I’ve used it for personal projects, studio classes, and research-based design work where iteration was essential. A skill I want to strengthen is front-end development, specifically React. I want to tighten the gap between design and implementation, plus this would be a big plus for me as a designer. I’m motivated by a desire to make things real; once I imagine something, I want to see it exist and work. That drive, plus a genuine love for learning and trying new experiences.
column26: I'm drawn to the expressive minimalism of Swiss and Typographic Style. Designers like Josef Müller-Brockmann and Armin Hofmann showed me that clarity doesn’t mean boring; it can be bold, and even rebellious. I’m especially inspired by how they used grid systems not to restrict, but to expand their creativity. In my own design, I often begin with strict limitations, and then find ways to bend or challenge them to create something unexpected but still grounded.

column23: My professional and extracurricular experiences have been central to my growth as both a designer and teammate. Last spring, I worked as a Junior Designer at Scout, where I helped design ZOR, a mobile app supporting individuals with epilepsy. It was my first time working on a full design team, and I learned a lot about collaboration, ownership, and designing with empathy. I also recently completed my first co-op, where I was the sole designer and developer of a mobile app for six months, a role that pushed me to develop strong time management and prioritization skills. In addition, my work at Knit Studios has given me the chance to work directly with clients on digital experiences, further strengthening my communication and project scoping skills.
column24: What sets me apart is my ability to bridge the gap between design and development. As a CS and Design major, I’ve had the opportunity to explore both disciplines in tandem, whether that means wireframing with developers in mind, or translating design decisions into component-based code. I’ve spent the past few years designing and building mobile apps, and I’ve developed a deep understanding of the constraints and patterns that shape mobile UX, like touch-based interactions, native navigation paradigms, and responsive motion. This technical empathy lets me design with intention and communicate clearly with cross-functional teammates.
column25: I’m very confident in Figma. It’s my go-to tool for everything from product design to quick visual mockups and even presentation decks. I know the tool inside and out and can work quickly while still keeping my files organized and developer-friendly. One skill I’d like to improve is vector illustration. I’ve dabbled with it throughout my time in HackBeanpot, but I’d love to feel more fluent when creating assets from scratch. I’m motivated to grow by being surrounded by people who challenge me. When I see someone do something I admire, I want to learn how they did it and push myself to try it too.
column26: I have to go with Jony Ive’s 2012 Apple era. The clean, minimal interfaces of iOS 7 really shaped how I think about digital design. I’m drawn to work that feels weightless, intuitive, and honest about its functionality. That influence shows up in my own designs through my focus on clarity, spatial hierarchy, and using motion sparingly but purposefully. It also comes through in my attention to detail. I care deeply about how every element contributes to the overall experience.

column23: I have recently interned at a software consulting company where I was a front-end developer. I worked with React and had hands-on experience with scrums and the Agile development architecture. This experience has greatly opened my eyes to consulting and allowed me to grow in the computer science and consulting space.
column24: I have participated in multiple hackathons and have led mini scrums in high school. I enjoy leading while also learning from others.
column25: I am great at front-end development and have used the React architecture for years but my backend knowledge is limited. I have worked with Java and Python but have only ever made a full backend for my database project (CS3200).
column26: I like industrial as it highlights the architecture the best. It is very brutal in showing everything without covering the structural and integral parts of a building.

column23: While I haven’t been in Generate yet, a lot of my growth has come from building things with people who care just as much as I do. Whether that’s designing a GUI for a marine robot in Northeastern Robotics or replacing an international nonprofit’s paper system with a clean Airtable dashboard. Each project taught me how to problem-solve in real-world contexts, communicate with both tech and non-tech people, and stay calm when things go wrong.   But beyond the code, what’s helped me grow the most is the people like the mentors, teammates, friends who made those experiences feel collaborative, not transactional. That’s what draws me to Generate. I want to build meaningful projects with people I can also grab a bagel with after a late-night bug fix. Growth to me is technical and personal, and I’ve heard Generate offers both in one very cool, very kind package.
column24: I approach every project with a focus on clarity, impact, and collaboration. I’ve worked across software, data, and finance, building systems that not only function well but serve the real needs of the teams using them. Whether I’m developing an analytics dashboard, writing backend logic, or refining documentation, I care about creating tools that are thoughtful, reliable, and easy to understand.   Unlike others, I have worked with engineers, marketers, professors, and nonprofit staff in Kenya, which taught me how to adapt fast, ask good questions, and keep things moving even when the answer isn’t in a textbook. But what really sets me apart is that I care: about the work, about the people I work with, and about making things better than how I found them. I show up ready to collaborate, experiment, and learn something new.
column25: One technical skill I feel confident in is writing clean, well-organized Java code. I’ve spent a lot of time learning how to structure my programs in a way that’s modular and maintainable, and I really enjoy the logic behind object-oriented design. It’s the kind of work that feels satisfying when everything fits together just right.   Something I’m less confident in is web development. I recently built my first full website and taught myself JavaScript, TailwindCSS, and a bit of backend with Flask. It was a fun challenge, but I know I’m still early in that journey. There’s so much more I want to learn, especially when it comes to building dynamic, user-friendly applications.   What motivates me most is the feeling of turning something unfamiliar into something I understand and then actually getting to use it to build things that work. I like pushing myself out of my comfort zone, especially when I know the skills I’m gaining will help me contribute more to a team. Plus, nothing beats the moment when something finally runs after hours of trial and error.
column26: I’m really drawn to modern minimalism, including clean layouts, intuitive interfaces, and a tech-forward feel. I like designs that feel simple and effortless, where everything flows naturally and nothing gets in the way. It’s a bit different from some people who prefer vintage or more decorative styles, which I can definitely appreciate, but modern minimalism just clicks with how I think about building things. Whether it’s designing a dashboard, organizing a layout, or even structuring my code, I focus on clarity, efficiency, and balance.

column23: Though I haven’t been in Generate before, I’ve been seeking out environments like it: collaborative, fast-paced, and creative. Working as a TA for both Discrete Structures and Database Design taught me how to support technical growth across different learning styles, while roles like my BSA co-op at UKG have shown me how crucial design thinking is during every stage of the development cycle. Projects through coursework and previous clubs have pushed me to combine backend systems with thoughtful user experience. These experiences taught me how to work across disciplines and adapt to challenges quickly, skills I’m excited to bring into Generate’s community.
column24: I’ve worked across a wide spectrum of environments, from writing SQL queries for classroom exercises to configuring Salesforce in a corporate sandbox. What sets me apart is my ability to move fluidly between systems thinking and a variety of designs. I’ve contributed towards DevOps for real-time changes to a sales database, taught complex logic to students as a TA, and prototyped full-stack apps with Docker containers. Each experience has pushed me to think critically, communicate clearly, and collaborate effectively. I also bring a mix of analytical thinking from my economics coursework and creativity from my design and painting hobbies. When working on a team, I know how to take initiative while still creating space for others’ ideas to thrive.
column25: I’m confident in my ability to break down and implement complex algorithms thanks to my coursework in Data Structures & Algorithms and Logic & Computation. I’ve applied concepts like Dijkstra’s, dynamic programming, and recursive problem solving in both class projects and side work like Three Trios. That said, I’d love to continue growing my frontend skills, especially in React and Figma, to design with a more complete view of the user journey. I’m motivated by the moment when something clicks and when a confusing concept suddenly makes sense or a system you built finally works. That feeling and the excitement of solving real problems with tech is what keeps me learning!
column26: I’ve always been someone who needs things to feel clean and put-together, whether it’s my desk, my calendar, or the way a page looks. That’s why I’m drawn to minimalist design and styles like Swiss Design. I like when there’s structure (grids, clear lines, good spacing), because it just makes everything easier to understand and more satisfying to look at. Even when I’m working on something technical like building a UI, I care a lot about how everything is laid out and how it feels to use. In Readly, for example, I paid close attention to keeping the interface simple, so nothing felt overwhelming. If something looks cluttered, it’s hard for me to feel good about it. Design, to me, should be clean, functional, and visually balanced, and I try to bring that mindset into everything I make (even when I bake lol).

column23: While I haven’t been part of Generate yet, my academic and professional journey has been shaped by experiences that mirror its values building, iterating, and solving real problems through technology.  As a Research Assistant at Northeastern, I’m working on a Swin Transformer-based deep learning model to detect malignant lung nodules from 3D CT scans. This isn't just a technical challenge it's a real-world problem with life or death stakes. It’s taught me how to balance innovation with rigor, and how to iterate fast while collaborating with domain experts like radiologists.  Before that, I helped scale an e-commerce platform to handle 700+ daily transactions, built a real-time financial dashboard that cut reconciliation time by 40%, and led machine learning efforts that boosted model accuracy to 96%. Across all of these, I’ve learned to work across disciplines, ship polished products, and own both the architecture and the impact.  I’m drawn to Generate because it blends creativity with accountability. That’s exactly the kind of environment where I thrive where ideas are meant to become products, and learning happens through doing.
column24: I bring a mix of research depth, engineering pragmatism, and team first mindset that helps me plug into any project and push it forward.  On the research side, I’ve designed and trained deep learning models like a Swin Transformer for lung cancer detection where precision and performance actually matter. I know how to work through ambiguity, experiment fast, and translate technical complexity into actionable steps.  But I’ve also built products in fast-paced settings: scalable e-commerce platforms, real-time data dashboards, and full-stack applications used by hundreds of people. That means I think beyond code—I care about uptime, users, and how things behave in the real world.  What ties it all together is how I show up in teams. I document well, debug collaboratively, and take initiative when things are unclear. Whether I’m leading a project or contributing quietly, my focus is on moving the team forward not just getting my part done.  That’s the combination I bring: research-grade thinking, builder’s instincts, and a teammate mindset.
column25: One technical skill I’m confident in is building end-to-end machine learning pipelines. From data cleaning and model training to deployment and monitoring, I’ve done it across research and real world applications. For instance, I recently optimized a PyTorch pipeline to train multiple Swin Transformer models for lung nodule detection. That taught me not just about model performance, but about reproducibility, collaboration, and iteration.  One skill I’d like to improve is systems design for large-scale distributed applications. I’ve built and deployed cloud-native apps using Docker, AWS, and FastAPI, but I want to deepen my understanding of how high-availability systems work at scale load balancing, sharding, fault tolerance, and all the architecture choices that power real world products.  What motivates me is impact. I like learning things that let me build better tools or solve harder problems. I’m drawn to challenges where the path isn’t obvious and you have to figure things out as you go. That’s where the real growth happens and honestly, that’s where it gets fun.
column26: I’m drawn to the Bauhaus design movement specifically its principle of “form follows function.” What I admire is how it strips things down to their purpose, then builds around that with clean lines, balance, and simplicity. It’s not flashy for the sake of being flashy it’s intentional.  That mindset influences how I approach both software and machine learning. Whether I’m designing a data pipeline, a FastAPI backend, or a model architecture, I try to keep things as simple and modular as possible. If something is overly complex, I question whether it really needs to be there.  Even when working on front-end interfaces or dashboards, I think about usability first. How will someone actually interact with this? Is it intuitive? Can we remove friction?  Bauhaus reminds me that good design isn’t about adding more it’s about making every part earn its place. That discipline helps me build systems that are not only effective but also maintainable and clear.

column23: As an incoming fourth year, I have had the opportunity to experience two different co-ops. These experiences have helped me shape where I want to be after graduation. Coming into co-op, I wasn't sure what exactly my desires were when it came to CS/Design, but being able to apply my skills in a professional setting allowed me to figure out my goals and strengths when it comes to these fields. Extracurricular / Professional experiences are a way for me to grow as an individual as well as grow within the community.
column24: I have always made it a priority to prioritize the user, and I believe my strong skillset and passion for human computer interaction would set me up for success in this role. I have strong communication skills and great teamwork skills, that I believe the combination of my passion in HCI and my overall team-driven efforts will allow me to collaborate with fellow Generate members to continue to build and scale our projects.
column25: I have developed a great skill for front-end development, specifically HTML, CSS, and JavaScript, however, I would love to continue improving my UX design through Figma. I consider my self intermediate when it comes to Figma, and it's definitely a skill I'd like to continue to grow in order to create better prototypes to share with my users.
column26: I am the most passionate about UX design principles and practices, however, I have recently been interested in Swiss Design, an ideology that incorporates grids and asymmetry in design. It has started to influence me to branch out more and not always be "by the book". I think the idea of asymmetry can be a good thing, but we are used to seeing symmetry in design.
